attributes.stem:
A 24-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with sudden onset right-sided lower abdominal pain that began two hours ago when exercising. The pain is severe and constant. She has vomited three times. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications except for the combined oral contraceptive pill. Her last menstrual period was two weeks ago. The pain has not eased despite simple and narcotic analgesia in ED.

On examination, she appears distressed. Her pulse rate is 105/min, blood pressure 125/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. Her abdomen is tender in the right iliac fossa with voluntary guarding. Transvaginal ultrasound reveals a 7 cm simple ovarian cyst on the right side. Doppler ultrasound demonstrates preserved blood flow to both ovaries. WCC and CRP are normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with clinical features highly suspicious for ovarian torsion, including sudden onset unilateral pelvic pain with associated nausea and vomiting. The presence of a large ovarian cyst is a significant risk factor. Despite the presence of blood flow on ultrasound, ovarian torsion cannot be excluded as it is primarily a clinical diagnosis.

Urgent laparoscopy is the most appropriate management as it allows both definitive diagnosis and treatment. Delay in surgical intervention can lead to ovarian necrosis and the need for oophorectomy. The presence of blood flow on ultrasound should not delay surgical intervention when clinical suspicion is high.

Discharge home with analgesia would be inappropriate given the severity of symptoms and high clinical suspicion for torsion. This could lead to delayed diagnosis and potential loss of ovarian function.

Arranging outpatient gynaecology follow-up fails to address the acute nature of the presentation and the need for urgent assessment. Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency requiring immediate intervention.

Repeat ultrasound in 24 hours would cause unnecessary delay in diagnosis and treatment. Ultrasound findings, including Doppler studies, can be falsely reassuring in cases of ovarian torsion.

Conservative management with observation would be inappropriate when there is high clinical suspicion for ovarian torsion. While this might be suitable for uncomplicated ovarian cysts, the acute presentation with severe pain and vomiting requires immediate surgical evaluation.

Studies have shown that normal blood flow can be present in up to 60% of cases of surgically confirmed ovarian torsion. This is because the venous and lymphatic drainage may be compromised while arterial flow is maintained, or because of intermittent torsion with spontaneous detorsion.
learningPoints: Ovarian torsion is a clinical diagnosis and cannot be excluded by the presence of blood flow on ultrasound.
The presence of a large ovarian cyst (>5cm) is a significant risk factor for ovarian torsion.
Sudden onset unilateral pelvic pain with associated nausea and vomiting should raise suspicion for ovarian torsion
references: Ashwal E, Krissi H, Hiersch L, Less S, Eitan R, Peled Y. Presentation, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Ovarian Torsion in Premenarchal Girls. J Pediatr Adolesc Gynecol. 2015 Dec;28(6):526-9. doi: 10.1016/j.jpag.2015.03.010. Epub 2015 Mar 28. PMID: 26168769.
Jones T. Ovarian torsion: an overview. O&G Magazine [Internet]. 2011 [cited 2024 Dec 4];13(1). Available from: https://www.ogmagazine.org.au/13/1-13/ovarian-torsion-an-overview/
